policy_statement: By 2030, ensure 100% of companies in the health sector implement disaster risk management plans that consider climate-change-related risks (A7e).
mechanism_description: Health-sector companies will have implemented disaster risk management plans that account for climate-change-related risks, improving organizational preparedness and adaptive capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard risk planning → ↑ preparedness/response capacity → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ climate-related damages/disruptions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human/system change: Health-sector companies develop and implement disaster risk management (DRM) plans explicitly incorporating climate-change-related risks (e.g., heatwaves, floods, wildfires, supply-chain shocks).  
(2) Mediating process: Risk assessment, early warning/trigger protocols, contingency planning, redundancy, training/drills, and coordinated response improve institutional readiness and decision-making under extreme events.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced disruption of healthcare services and reduced losses (economic, health, operational) during and after climate-related hazards—i.e., increased adaptive capacity and resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced impacts from climate-related hazards (e.g., fewer service disruptions, damages, and health-system failures) through improved preparedness and response.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism targets preparedness and institutional capacity to manage climate-related disasters, thereby reducing vulnerability and damages from hazards.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: It does not primarily change energy/material use (Resource Efficiency), increase carbon sinks (Nature-Based Solutions), or directly cut emissions (Mitigation); its main effect is resilience to climate impacts.  
3) Edge cases applied: DRM plans can incidentally include mitigation co-benefits (e.g., backup power efficiency), but those are secondary; the core intent and expected effect is risk reduction and continuity under climate extremes.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy whose mechanism is “DRM plans → institutional adaptive capacity” is fundamentally about improving preparedness/response systems, which is adaptation across contexts and sectors.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., heat, flood, wildfire; health-system continuity; supply-chain resilience) depends on policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a DRM plan mandate is bundled with substantial hardening/retrofitting or low-carbon backup power requirements, those components could introduce mitigation or resource-efficiency pathways, but the canonical mechanism as stated remains primarily adaptation.
key_indicators: “By 2030”; “100% of companies in the health sector”; “implemented disaster risk management plans”; “consideration of risks resulting from climate change”
co_benefits: Improved general emergency preparedness for non-climate disasters; stronger business continuity and operational risk management
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via a regulatory mandate or accreditation requirement in practice, but the text itself specifies an outcome target (100% adoption) without stating the enforcement/compliance instrument.
